attributes.title: Setting Up Git
attributes.description: Install Git and configure your identity so your work is properly tracked.

post.html:
Installing Git

Git needs to be installed on your system before we can use it.






Linux (Ubuntu / Debian-based)

sudo apt update
sudo apt install git

If you are using another Linux distribution, use your package manager (e.g., dnf, pacman, zypper).






Windows

Download Git from the official website:

👉 https://git-scm.com/

During installation:

  • Use the default options
  • Choose Git Bash when prompted

📌 Use Git Bash on Windows — it gives you a Unix-like terminal experience similar to Linux






Verify the Installation

After installation, open a terminal (or Git Bash on Windows) and run:

git --version

Example output:

➜  ~ git --version
git version 2.43.0
➜  ~

If you see a version number, Git is installed correctly.

If something goes wrong

  • Restart your terminal
  • Make sure Git is added to your system PATH
  • On Windows, ensure you are using Git Bash






Configure Git (Very Important)

Before using Git, we must tell it who you are.

Git records your name and email with every commit you make.

Git does not verify that this name or email belongs to you—it simply records the information you provide.

Run the following commands:

git config --global user.name "John Doe"
git config --global user.email "john@example.com"

Replace these values with your own information.

📌 Important

  • This does not create a GitHub account
  • This only identifies your commits locally






Check Your Configuration

  • To verify your settings, run:

    git config user.name
git config user.email

  • You should see something like:

    ➜  ~ git config user.name
Shafayetul Huda Sadi
➜  ~ git config user.email
shafayet.sadi@gmail.com
➜  ~

  • That means Git knows who you are.

To see all your Git configurations, run:

git config --list

📌 On some systems, Git may open the output in a pager. If that happens, press q to exit.






Global vs Local Configuration

Git configurations can be set at different levels.

Global configuration

  • Applies to all repositories on your computer
  • This is what we just set
  • Most of us only need this

Local configuration

  • Applies to one specific repository
  • Overrides global settings for that project

📌 For now, global configuration is enough.






Why Configuring Git Matters

Configuring Git helps you:

  • Identify who made each change
  • Understand project history clearly
  • Ensure your commits are correctly attributed to you
  • Collaborate without confusion

Every commit tells a story — your name is part of that story.






Quick Check

Before moving on, make sure:

  • git --version works
  • git config user.name shows your name
  • git config user.email shows your email

If all three work, you're ready for your first repository.
post.markdown:
## Installing Git Git needs to be installed on your system before we can use it.




### Linux (Ubuntu / Debian-based) ```bash sudo apt update sudo apt install git ``` > If you are using another Linux distribution, use your package manager > (e.g., `dnf`, `pacman`, `zypper`).




### Windows Download Git from the official website: 👉 [https://git-scm.com/](https://git-scm.com/) During installation: - Use the **default options** - Choose **Git Bash** when prompted 📌 Use **Git Bash** on Windows — it gives you a Unix-like terminal experience similar to Linux




## Verify the Installation After installation, open a terminal (or Git Bash on Windows) and run: ```bash git --version ``` Example output: ```bash ➜ ~ git --version git version 2.43.0 ➜ ~ ``` If you see a version number, Git is installed correctly. ### If something goes wrong - Restart your terminal - Make sure Git is added to your system PATH - On Windows, ensure you are using **Git Bash**




## Configure Git (Very Important) Before using Git, we must tell it **who you are**. Git records your name and email with every commit you make. Git does not verify that this name or email belongs to you—it simply records the information you provide. Run the following commands: ```bash git config --global user.name "John Doe" git config --global user.email "john@example.com" ``` Replace these values with your own information. 📌 **Important** - This does **not** create a GitHub account - This only identifies your commits locally




## Check Your Configuration - To verify your settings, run: ```bash git config user.name git config user.email ``` - You should see something like: ```text ➜ ~ git config user.name Shafayetul Huda Sadi ➜ ~ git config user.email shafayet.sadi@gmail.com ➜ ~ ``` - That means Git knows who you are. To see all your Git configurations, run: ```bash git config --list ``` 📌 On some systems, Git may open the output in a pager. If that happens, press `q` to exit.




## Global vs Local Configuration Git configurations can be set at different levels. ### Global configuration - Applies to **all repositories** on your computer - This is what we just set - Most of us only need this ### Local configuration - Applies to **one specific repository** - Overrides global settings for that project 📌 For now, **global configuration is enough**.




## Why Configuring Git Matters Configuring Git helps you: - Identify who made each change - Understand project history clearly - Ensure your commits are correctly attributed to you - Collaborate without confusion > Every commit tells a story — your name is part of that story.




## Quick Check Before moving on, make sure: - `git --version` works - `git config user.name` shows your name - `git config user.email` shows your email If all three work, you're ready for your first repository.
